caveats: a: OEWS covers all NAICS 423100, not used-parts wholesalers specifically.
a: LKQ is a very large, technologically mature public operator and is not representative of a typical LMM recycler.
a: Occupation shares and examples of current software do not directly measure wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure.

rho: Computer vision cannot reliably certify hidden damage or remaining life without inspection and testing.
rho: Data rights and access restrictions for newer software-defined vehicles may impede matching and diagnostics.

e: The dataset n is margin-bridged and may misclassify asset-heavy yards with volatile scrap and salvage economics.

s5: Announced strategic acquisitions overrepresent scaled and compliant operators.
s5: Asset-only yard or real-estate sales, closures, and internal reorganizations are not qualifying transfers.

q: Salvage-auction prices, scrap commodities, collision frequency, and insurer policy can swamp workflow gains.
q: Large networks may retain more through breadth and fulfillment than independent local yards.
d5: EPA's national salvage-flow figure is old and includes vehicles ultimately processed mainly as scrap.
d5: Vehicle stock, age, and miles do not directly measure used-parts purchases.
d5: LKQ's scale and product mix extend beyond 423140 and beyond the US.
d5: Software locks, sensor calibration, battery economics, and structural casting can reduce reuse of some newer-vehicle components.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repair and collision customers buy specific used parts as lower-cost alternatives to new OEM or aftermarket parts; the operator earns part margin plus residual scrap and core value and may provide search, interchange matching, condition grading, removal, warehousing, delivery, returns, warranty, and estimating-system integration.
lens.excluded_activities: Retail-only self-service junkyards, scrap-only wreckers classified outside used-parts wholesaling, auction agents, rebuilders selling complete vehicles, captive internal units, shells, internal reorganizations, and firms lacking transferable operating, compliance, inventory, and customer systems.
lens.included_activities: Independent US wholesale automotive recyclers and salvage-yard operators that acquire end-of-life or total-loss vehicles, depollute and dismantle them, identify and inventory reusable parts, and repeatedly sell and deliver used collision, mechanical, body, electrical, and major components to repair shops, dealers, rebuilders, fleets, and other business customers.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation employment and wages for broad NAICS 423100, not wage-weighted tasks in 423140. I map portions of salvage-vehicle bidding, inventory-demand analysis, image and document processing, interchange matching, parts identification and cataloging, pricing, quoting, customer service, order entry, and dispatch to AI exposure while leaving depollution, dismantling, grading, warehouse movement, delivery, and accountable exception handling mostly unexposed. The broad group may understate 423140's manual yard labor or understate its unusually data-intensive one-off inventory matching.
caveats: OEWS covers all NAICS 423100, not used-parts wholesalers specifically.
LKQ is a very large, technologically mature public operator and is not representative of a typical LMM recycler.
Occupation shares and examples of current software do not directly measure wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task study across 15-20 independent recyclers, measuring acquisition, inspection, depollution, dismantling, imaging, cataloging, interchange, pricing, sales, yard, delivery, returns, warranty, compliance, and work already automated.
a.rationale: The 423100 proxy reports 19.66% of employment in sales and 13.83% in office/administrative support, against 29.96% transportation/material-moving and 16.10% installation/repair. Direct operating evidence shows that a large recycler already uses software for vehicle bid pricing, inventory, parts entry, repair-estimator synchronization, search, pricing, ordering, and routing. AI can extend those information tasks, but each unique salvage vehicle still requires physical inspection, safe fluid removal, dismantling, condition judgment, storage, and movement.

d5.bridge: EPA's annual end-of-life vehicle flow, DOE vehicle-age observations, FHWA vehicle stock and miles, and LKQ's business disclosures are supply and demand drivers rather than a five-year constant-price forecast for 423140. I combine over 10 million vehicles entering salvage/scrap annually, an old vehicle fleet, 297.5 million registered vehicles and 3.294 trillion miles in 2024, and recurring collision/mechanical repair demand into a modest-growth judgment. This may overstate wholesale demand if vehicles are exported, rebuilt, scrapped without parts recovery, or routed to retail/self-service channels and understate it if repair-cost inflation increases total-loss and alternative-parts use.
caveats: EPA's national salvage-flow figure is old and includes vehicles ultimately processed mainly as scrap.
Vehicle stock, age, and miles do not directly measure used-parts purchases.
LKQ's scale and product mix extend beyond 423140 and beyond the US.
Software locks, sensor calibration, battery economics, and structural casting can reduce reuse of some newer-vehicle components.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of repairable claims, total-loss vehicles, salvage acquisition units, recovered saleable parts, vehicle-age/powertrain mix, insurer alternative-part usage, and independent wholesale channel share, all in constant-quality units.
d5.rationale: Used-parts demand is supported by collision and mechanical repairs on a large, aging vehicle population, while supply is replenished by more than 10 million vehicles sent annually to salvage yards and scrap facilities. DOE reports 2023 average ages of 13.1 years for pickups, 12.1 for heavy trucks, 11.9 for cars, and 11.8 for vans. Five-year quantity should remain durable, but more complex parts, EV mix, OEM software restrictions, repairability, insurer policy, auction supply, and direct digital channels widen the range.

caveats: Public sources define activities but do not measure the eligible LMM firm share.
NAICS boundaries between wholesale used parts, retail junkyards, and scrap wholesalers may be imperfect in business records.
The dataset n is margin-bridged and may misclassify asset-heavy yards with volatile scrap and salvage economics.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level screen of the dataset-estimated LMM population using wholesale versus retail revenue, repeat B2B customer share, parts versus scrap revenue, ultimate ownership, environmental and title compliance, yard ownership, employee depth, inventory-system quality, concentration, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: The NAICS activity itself combines acquisition, dismantling, inventory, and wholesale sale of used parts, and many operators repeatedly serve repair shops, dealers, and rebuilders. Eligibility is reduced by retail self-service yards, scrap-first operators, thin owner-dependent yards, environmental or title liabilities, poor inventory systems, customer concentration, and businesses whose value is principally land or commodity scrap rather than transferable recurring operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423140 ($1,414,954,086) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423140 ($14,873,464,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $649,723 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423140); class <5: 724 firms, avg revenue/firm $828,535 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $94,204 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 299 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,500,913 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $170,654 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 174 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,347,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $494,311 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 146 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,464,925 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,303,562 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,474,657 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,351,269 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $575,715,278 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,458,827 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 181

caveats: Some accountable functions may be separated among auctions, dismantlers, marketplaces, logistics firms, and warranty providers.
Automation may reduce headcount materially without eliminating the operator entity.
Environmental and title rules vary by state and can change operating requirements.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on end-to-end robotic depollution and dismantling, reliable computer-vision and diagnostic certification of used parts, platform guarantees, direct yard-to-repair fulfillment, and the share of transactions completed without an integrated accountable recycler.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five quantity requires an accountable operator to acquire and title salvage vehicles, depollute them, physically dismantle and inspect parts, determine condition and interchange, store unique inventory, fulfill and deliver, manage warranties and returns, and comply with environmental rules. Software can automate discovery, matching, pricing, and ordering and may shift transactions to platforms, but it cannot itself recover or certify the physical component or manage fluids, hazardous materials, yards, and hulks.

caveats: No observed study isolates retention of AI-generated benefit.
Salvage-auction prices, scrap commodities, collision frequency, and insurer policy can swamp workflow gains.
Large networks may retain more through breadth and fulfillment than independent local yards.
q.change_evidence: Part-level cohorts before and after automation showing acquisition allocation, recovery yield, labor, days in stock, price realization, returns, warranty claims, freight, repair-cycle time, customer retention, insurer acceptance, and competing quotes.
q.rationale: Used parts are heterogeneous, locally scarce, and condition-specific, so better salvage bidding, recovery selection, catalog accuracy, matching, pricing, and routing can improve margin and turns without an automatic contractual pass-through. Repair shops and insurers value lower repair cost and cycle time, but estimating-system integration and e-commerce make alternatives more comparable, insurers influence part choice, and auction competition raises input cost. The operator should retain about half of implemented benefit in the base case.

caveats: No public 423140 study measures five-year AI implementation.
Computer vision cannot reliably certify hidden damage or remaining life without inspection and testing.
Data rights and access restrictions for newer software-defined vehicles may impede matching and diagnostics.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after production studies from independent recyclers deploying AI in acquisition, vision grading, cataloging, interchange, pricing, quoting, routing, and compliance, with labor hours, error/return rates, overrides, recovery yield, fill rate, claims, and implementation cost.
rho.rationale: Over five years, implementable use cases include salvage-bid support, demand and price prediction, computer-vision-assisted identification and damage grading, interchange suggestions, listing creation, quote and order automation, customer-service triage, delivery planning, and compliance-document extraction. Current industry software already performs several precursor functions. Implementation is limited by unique and dirty inventory, incomplete interchange data, safety and warranty risk, legacy yard systems, physical validation, environmental compliance, and the integration capacity of small operators.

caveats: No public 423140 control-transfer rate was found.
Announced strategic acquisitions overrepresent scaled and compliant operators.
Asset-only yard or real-estate sales, closures, and internal reorganizations are not qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed automotive-recycler acquisitions and asset sales from industry brokers, SEC filings, and private deal databases, coded for control and operating continuity, plus an owner survey on succession, compliance capital, intended exit date, and willingness to sell.
s5.rationale: A somewhat-above-one-fifth five-year qualifying transfer probability is a bounded judgment. Aging owner-operators, environmental and technology investment needs, insurer and auction scale, and ongoing consolidation can motivate sales. Against that, small yards may close, sell real estate or inventory, transfer within families, or fail environmental and title diligence rather than transfer as a going concern.

principal_driver: AI can improve valuation and matching across unique salvage vehicles and parts while an aging fleet sustains repair demand.
principal_weakness: The eligible population is small and operationally risky, and much of the labor remains physical dismantling, inspection, handling, and delivery.

caveats: S1 is a Census questionnaire rather than observed industry results, so it supports classification and relevant dimensions but not the prevalence of transferable operations or exposed work.

The a estimate maps broad NAICS 423100 occupation headcounts to wage-weighted, not-yet-automated tasks; both the industry mismatch and transformation uncertainty are disclosed, but the resulting 0.18-0.37 range remains judgment-heavy.

LKQ is a scaled, diversified, technologically mature operator spanning products and geographies beyond 423140, limiting its representativeness for independent LMM recyclers even though its cited operating disclosures are accurate.

The more-than-10-million EPA flow is an old combined salvage-and-scrap statistic and cannot establish current saleable-parts throughput; DOE vehicle ages and FHWA stock/miles likewise indicate context rather than a five-year constant-quality demand forecast.

The lens centers integrated recyclers and dismantling salvage yards, while the Census form permits a broader used-parts merchant-wholesale population. This coherent operational focus may underrepresent non-integrated distributors and could shift a, e, and o under another defensible lens, but the record does not provide evidence that the correction would change its base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

The numeric bases for rho, e, s5, q, and o remain bounded judgments without direct industry measurements. Their ESTIMATE labels, ranges, caveats, and change-evidence requests make the uncertainty visible, so this is not a material evidence-state defect.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 Census questionnaire supports the 423140 classification, the distinction between wholesale used-parts dealers/wholesale junkyards and retail or scrap activities, and the presence of inventory, customer, selling-method, employee-function, and technology questions. It is a blank collection instrument, not evidence of how prevalent those operating characteristics are or of the scored exposure and eligibility values.

The May 2023 OEWS page reports 367,580 jobs in broad NAICS 423100 and the cited shares of 19.66% sales, 13.83% office/administrative support, 16.10% installation/repair, and 29.96% transportation/material-moving employment. The packet explicitly identifies the broader industry and task-mapping limitations.

LKQ's 2025 Form 10-K supports the stated operating claims: acquisition and dismantling of total-loss vehicles, bid-pricing software, parts inventory and repair-estimator synchronization, search/price/order tools, routing software, repair-shop customers, insurer influence, warranties, constrained salvage supply, software-related reuse restrictions, and physical distribution. It does not directly measure the packet's AI implementation, eligibility, retention, demand, or operator-share estimates.

The archived EPA page states that over 10 million vehicles are sent to salvage yards and scrap facilities annually and that about 75% of vehicle weight is recycled metal. The packet appropriately notes that this old combined flow is not a current measure of recoverable wholesale-parts supply.

The EPA end-of-life vehicle guide documents hazardous-fluid removal, dismantling for usable or recyclable parts, hulk storage and movement, and sale of salvageable engine and body components to repair shops or restorers. It supports the physical and compliance-intensive workflow claims, not a quantitative five-year automation rate.

The DOE page reports the cited 2023 average ages: 13.1 years for pickups, 12.1 for heavy trucks, 11.9 for cars, 11.8 for vans, and 8.1 for SUVs/CUVs. These are correctly used as a disclosed demand proxy rather than a direct used-parts forecast.

FHWA Table FI-200 reports 297,525,836 registered vehicles and 3,294,031 million vehicle-miles in 2024, versus 276,491,174 vehicles and 3,261,772 million miles in 2019, supporting the packet's rounded stock and mileage claims. Those measures do not directly quantify used-parts demand, as the bridge acknowledges.

sources.supports: Identifies used-parts dealers including wholesale junkyards; distinguishes own-account merchant wholesalers from retail yards and other operating types; asks about inventory, e-commerce, customers, employee functions, and automation technologies.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form: Used Motor Vehicle Parts and Supplies

sources.supports: Reports 367,580 total jobs; 19.66% sales, 13.83% office/administrative support, 16.10% installation/maintenance/repair, and 29.96% transportation/material-moving employment, with detailed occupations and wages.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 423100

sources.supports: Describes acquiring and dismantling total-loss vehicles, software-supported salvage bidding, parts inventory and repair-estimator integration, e-commerce search/price/order tools, repair-shop customers, insurer influence, routing, warranties, limited salvage supply, technology restrictions, and physical distribution.
sources.title: LKQ Corporation 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: States that more than 10 million vehicles are sent to salvage yards and scrap facilities each year and approximately 75% of a vehicle by weight is metal that is recycled.
sources.title: US EPA Vehicles Product Stewardship

sources.supports: Details vehicle acceptance and storage, hazardous-material removal, dismantling for usable parts, hulk storage and crushing; states that usable engine and body components can be salvaged, reconditioned, and sold to repair shops or restorers.
sources.title: Processing End-of-Life Vehicles: A Guide for Environmental Protection, Safety and Profit

sources.supports: Reports 2023 average ages of 13.1 years for pickup trucks, 12.1 for heavy trucks, 11.9 for cars, 11.8 for vans, and 8.1 for SUVs/CUVs, based on Argonne analysis of vehicles-in-operation data.
sources.title: DOE Fact of the Week 1362: Average Vehicle Age by Type, 2023

sources.supports: Reports 297,525,836 registered vehicles and 3.294 trillion vehicle-miles in 2024, compared with 276,491,174 registered vehicles and 3.262 trillion miles in 2019.

title: Motor Vehicle Parts (Used) Merchant Wholesalers
